question_text: Explain why a runner's average speed over a 100-metre sprint might be less than their maximum speed during the race.
correct_answer: The runner starts from rest and accelerates during the first part of the race, so their speed during this acceleration phase is less than their maximum speed. Since average speed is calculated over the entire distance/time, including the slower acceleration phase, it is lower than the peak speed reached later in the race.
explanation: Average speed is the total distance divided by total time, while maximum (instantaneous) speed is the fastest speed reached at any moment. If a runner accelerates gradually from rest, they spend time moving slower than their peak speed, which brings down the average. The runner would only have an average speed equal to their maximum speed if they somehow maintained that top speed for the entire 100 metres, which is not realistic.
